milestone.title: Reading and Writing Files
summary: Read file contents with cat, head, and tail
Write and append to files with > and >>
milestone.nextLevelTeaser: Next: Level 4 — Your Code Has a History. Learn git and never lose your work again.

sections.content: Level 2 complete! You've learned to read, write, search, and chain commands together. Let's test your recall on all the file content tools you've mastered.
sections.analogy: You've built up a full toolkit for working with file contents — reading glasses (`cat`, `head`, `tail`), a pen (`echo`, `>`, `>>`), a magnifying glass (`grep`), and assembly-line connectors (`|`). Let's see if you can pick the right tool for each job.
keyPoints: Reading: `cat` for full files, `head`/`tail` for previews
Searching: `grep` finds patterns, `-i` ignores case, `-r` searches recursively
Chaining: `|` pipes one command's output into another

sections.instruction: Match each command to what it does.

pairs.right: Shows the last lines of a file

sections.question: What's the key difference between `>` and `>>`?
options: `>` replaces the entire file; `>>` adds to the end
sections.explanation: `>` overwrites — everything in the file is replaced. `>>` appends — new content is added to the end, preserving what was already there. Both create the file if it doesn't exist.

sections.prompt: To search for 'TODO' in every file in every subfolder, you type: grep ___ "TODO" .

sections.instruction: Categorize each tool by what it's primarily used for.

sections.question: Which command pipeline counts the number of lines containing 'ERROR' in `server.log`?
sections.explanation: First, `grep ERROR server.log` finds all lines containing 'ERROR'. Then `| wc -l` counts those lines. The pipe sends grep's output as input to wc. This is the power of command chaining.
